content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 13, 2016

Donald A. Larkin
City Attorney
City of Morgan Hill
17575 Peak Avenue
Morgan Hill, CA 95037

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-227

Dear Mr. Larkin:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Councilmember Larry Carr
regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note
that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under
other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section
1090. Moreover, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission
does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

May Councilmember Carr take part in decisions to make recommendations to the High
Speed Rail Authority (the "Rail Authority") regarding the proposed High Speed Rail that will pass
through the city where his condominium may be impacted by noise and traffic and may be taken by
eminent domain?

CONCLUSION

No. Councilmember Carr may not take part in these decisions because the decisions will
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his real property.

FACTS

You are the City Attorney for the City of Morgan Hill and are requesting advice on behalf of
the City Council and Councilmember Carr.

___________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-227
Page No. 2

The Rail Authority has proposed that the state's High Speed Rail will travel through the City of Morgan Hill. In its 2008 and 2012 program-level Environmental Impact Reports, the Rail Authority studied a rail alignment that would use the existing Union Pacific right-of-way. Councilmember Carr lives in and owns a condominium unit within 500 feet of the existing rail corridor. When the Rail Authority conducts the project-level environmental review, the Rail Authority may also consider other alignments - the Highway 101 corridor or a route that would be adjacent to the existing Union Pacific right-of-way.

Before the Rail Authority makes decisions regarding rail alignment, frequency of train operation and the design of the railway system it is expected that the Rail Authority will request the City's input regarding environmental review, community expectations and other issues relating to the project as it comes through Morgan Hill. The City will have no control over any aspect of the project.

Councilmember Carr's property may be impacted by the High Speed Rail if the tracks are built on or near the Union Pacific right-of-way. The potential impacts include increased noise from the increased frequency of trains and potential increased traffic due to the removal of grade crossings. In addition, if the Rail Authority is unable to access the Union Pacific right-of-way, it could choose to align the railway adjacent to the existing rail line. This may require the Rail Authority to acquire a portion of his condominium's common area by eminent domain.

Based on preliminary information from the Rail Authority and a review of GIS² information, train noise will impact well over twenty-five percent (25%) of all real property within the City regardless of alignment. You believe that any noise or traffic impacts on Councilmember Carr's property would not be unique. However, unlike the other properties, there is a possibility that his property would be taken by eminent domain.

ANALYSIS

Under Section 87100, the general rule is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes, participates in making, or uses his or her position to influence a governmental decision which has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests.

You have identified one interest that may result in a conflict of interest for Councilmember Carr – a real property interest in his condominium unit.³

Foreseeability

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. The standard for foreseeability

_________________________

² Geographic Information System.

³ Regulation 18702.2(d)(4) provides: Real property in which an official has a financial interest does not include any common area as part of the official's ownership interest in a common interest development as defined in the Davis-Stirling Common Interest Development Act (Civil Code Sections 4000 et seq.).

File No. A-16-227
Page No. 3

differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. Under the facts you
have provided, Councilmember Carr's property is not explicitly involved in Rail Authority
decisions. Thus, as applied to his property, Regulation 18701(b) provides: "In general, if the
financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical,
it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary
circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable."

Materiality

As relevant to your facts, the financial effect of the City Council decisions on
Councilmember Carr's real property if the decisions:

• Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially altering traffic
levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property surrounding the official's real
property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other
factors that would affect the market value of the real property parcel in which the official
has a financial interest (Regulation 18702.2(a)(10);

• Would consider any decision affecting real property value located within 500 feet of the
property line of the official's real property ... Notwithstanding this prohibition, the
Commission may provide written advice allowing an official to participate under these
circumstances if the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to indicate that
there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's property
(Regulation 18702.2(a)(11);

• Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration under the
circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a nature that its
reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of the official's property
(Regulation 18702.2(a) 12).

You state that if the tracks are built on or near the existing Union Pacific right-of-way,
which is located less than 500 feet from Councilmember Carr's property, there would potentially be
increased noise and traffic impacts on his property. It is likely that this would have a negative
impact on the value of his property

You also state that if the Railway Authority is unable to access the Union Pacific right-of-
way, it may choose to align the railway adjacent to the existing rail line and that this could require
the Rail Authority to acquire his property by eminent domain. Eminent domain is the power of a
government to purchase private property for a "public use" so long as the property owner is paid
"just compensation" which is defined as the fair market value of the property. If the property owner
and the government disagree on the fair market value or if the property owner opposes the
purported "public use," the dispute is determined by a court of law. It is readily apparent that once a
government agency makes public its intent to acquire property by eminent domain, the property
owner's ability to sell the property is severely limited and would affect the price the owner could
command. (Buyers would hesitate to assume the risk of receiving less than the buyer believes is fair
market value.) Under these facts, the reasonably foreseeable financial effects of City Council
decisions regarding the High Speed Rail on Councilmember Carr's property are material.

File No. A-16-227
Page No. 4

Appearance as a Member of the General Public

Regulation 18704 sets forth the definitions of "making a decision," "participating in a
decision," and "using official position to attempt to influence a decision" for purposes of the Act.
Regulation 18704(d)(2)(A) establishes an exception to those definitions and provides as follows:

"(d) Exceptions. Making, participating in making, or influencing a
governmental decision does not include: …

"(2) Appearances as a Member of the General Public. An appearance by a
public official as a member of the general public before an agency in the
course of its prescribed governmental function if the official is appearing
on matters related solely to the his or her personal interests, including
interests in: …

"(A) Real property owned entirely by the official, members of his or her
immediate family, or the official and members of his or her immediate
family.[.]"

Under these provisions, Councilmember Carr may appear as a member of the general public
before the City Council in the course of its prescribed governmental function if he is appearing on
matters solely related to his personal interests in his residence and if his residence is entirely owned
by him, members of his immediate family, or him and members of his immediate family.⁴

To avail himself of this exception, Councilmember Carr must follow the procedures set forth
in Regulation 18707. He must publicly identify each type of financial interest involved in the
decision that gives rise to the disqualifying conflict of interest. The identification must be made
after the announcement of the agenda item to be discussed or voted on, but before the discussion or
vote commences. He must also recuse himself from voting on the matter, leave the dais to speak
from the same area as the members of the public and limit his remarks solely to the official's
personal interests.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division

VJ:jgl

⁴ If Councilmember Carr wishes to address the Rail Authority, he may do so provided that he does so in his
personal capacity and does not purport to act within his official authority or on behalf of his agency. (Regulation
18704(c)(2).)

have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his real property.

Speed Rail Authority (the "Rail Authority") regarding the proposed High Speed Rail that will pass 
through the city where his condominium may be impacted by noise and traffic and may be taken by 
eminent domain?

CONCLUSION: No. Councilmember Carr may not take part in these decisions because the decisions will
